relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Job answered Bildad.
He asks how long they will crush his soul with words.
Again and again they insult him without shame.
Even if he erred, the error would be his to carry.
They magnify themselves by using his disgrace.
Job says the Divine has bent him and surrounded him with a net.
He cries violence and receives no justice.
His way is walled, his paths darkened.
Glory and crown are stripped away.
Hope is uprooted like a tree.
He feels counted as an adversary.
Troops seem camped around his tent.
Brothers and acquaintances are far away.
Relatives fail and companions forget.
Even his household sees him as a stranger.
He calls, but even servants do not answer.
His breath and appeal are strange to his own family.
Children reject and speak against him.
Those he loved turn against him.
He survives by the thinnest edge.
He begs his friends for pity, because the hand of the Divine has touched him.
He asks why they pursue him too.
He longs for his words to be written.
He wants them engraved forever in rock.
Yet he knows his vindicator lives and will stand at last upon the dust.
Beyond bodily ruin, he longs to see the Divine.
He wants his own eyes to behold, not as a stranger.
He warns the friends not to keep hunting the root of blame in him.
They too should fear judgment.
